data.title: Netflix: Three Reasons To Expect An Earnings Beat
data.description:
Introduction

Netflix, Inc. (NFLX) is the world’s leading streaming platform with over 325 million paid subscribers across more than 190 countries. The company primarily derives its revenue through subscriptions with three pricing tiers (ad-supported, Standard, and Premium). This subscription revenue is complemented by a rapidly growing advertising business that is becoming a meaningful contributor to overall revenue.

Despite macroeconomic headwinds from tariffs and geopolitical turmoil pressuring the consumer, Netflix continues to deliver a value proposition that is difficult for its customer base to relinquish. However, the market acknowledges this risk and has punished the stock in anticipation of weaker performance, with shares down 26% from ATH.

With 1Q26 earnings on April 16th, 2026, I believe Netflix’s recent share price drop creates an attractive entry point for investors. In addition, I see three specific tailwinds that will assist Netflix in delivering an earnings beat and full-year guidance raise: a recently announced price increase, accelerating advertising revenue supported by an in-house ad tech stack, and a $2.8 billion breakup fee. As a result, I rate NFLX a Buy.

Business Overview

Netflix derives its revenue from two primary streams: subscriptions and advertising. While there is not a segment breakdown, there is clear commentary from management. In 2025, advertising revenue grew 2.5x to approximately $1.5 billion. For 2026, management has guided to a rough doubling of that figure to approximately $3 billion.

As per Netflix Co-CEO Gregory K. Peters on their 4Q25 Earnings Call:

Ad sales, 2.5x in 2025. We expect that business to roughly double again in 2026 to about $3 billion... So we're making good progress and the opportunity ahead of us is massive. We are still under 10% of TV time in all major markets which we compete. We've got hundreds of millions of households around the world still to sign up. We're just about 7% of the addressable market in terms of consumer and ad spend. "

While their ad revenue business was only created 3 years ago, it is quickly becoming a meaningful contributor to total revenue. Today, ad revenue represents 3.3% of revenue ($1.5 billion of $45.2 billion in 2025). At the guided $3 billion run rate against an estimated $51.2 billion in 2026 revenue, ad revenues would represent 5.9% of total revenue. In addition, NFLX has built its own proprietary ad infrastructure, which launched in late 2025, reducing dependency on other companies.

By owning the full technology stack, Netflix commands direct control over inventory pricing, targeting capabilities, and margins, improving unit economics as the business continues to scale.

Churn

On March 26, 2026, NFLX announced a price increase: ad-supported will be increased by $1, with standard and premium increasing by $2. A common concern against NFLX’s price increases is churn: the expectation that price increases will result in a permanent decrease in subscribers. Fortunately, the data for Netflix suggests otherwise.

According to third-party research from Antenna, as many as 44% of churned Netflix subscribers resubscribe within 12 months. This highlights NFLX’s platform stickiness and their ability to raise prices as needed.

This is also highlighted by their value proposition. As per Moffett Nathanson’s equity research, Netflix delivers the lowest cost per hour of entertainment relative to other major streaming platforms. According to their research, NFLX’s revenue per hour is only 48 cents, relative to 64-93 cents per hour for Disney+, Peacock, HBO Max, and Paramount+. This assessment implies that Netflix provides the cheapest hour of entertainment in streaming.

This has allowed NFLX to raise their subscription prices at a low-to-mid single-digit CAGR in the past 15 years. As per Co-CEO Gregory Peters, he sees ample opportunity in the future for further price increases.

Finally, we have to recognize that subscriber churn is not necessarily a win or lose scenario. A subscriber downgrading from the Premium Plan ($26.99) to the ad-supported tier ($8.99) does not represent a complete loss to NFLX. They still remain in the NFLX ecosystem and continue to generate revenue due to ad sales. As per Antenna, there is also a strong chance of said consumer re-upgrading to the Premium Plan.

ARPU

To illustrate the opportunity from NFLX’s 2026 price increases, I’ve constructed a baseline using NFLX’s reported blended U.S. ARPU of $17.26. NFLX last reported this number in their 2024 8-K.

While NFLX does not disclose U.S. subscriber tier composition, I reverse engineered a blended ARPU that is similar to their reported figure to establish a baseline. This composition is 40% ad-supported, 30% standard, and 30% Premium. In addition, I modelled an expected churn of 2%.

This results in approximately $1.28 billion in net incremental annual revenue. Content costs do not scale with a price increase, meaning most of this incremental revenue will flow through to operating income, supporting margin expansion. If NFLX were able to realize $1.28 billion in net incremental revenue as operating income, this alone would result in a 9.6% increase from their 2025 operating income, strengthening the possibility of a beat and raise from NFLX.

WBD Breakup

On February 27, 2026, Netflix conceded to Paramount (PSKY) and walked away from its pursuit of Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD), collecting a $2.8 billion breakup fee. At the same time, NFLX announced the resumption of its share buyback program. NFLX had previously guided for a 50 basis point operating margin contraction due to anticipated M&A-related expenses. Now that they’re unencumbered by their WBD-related obligations, Netflix can drive free cash flow toward content production and continued share repurchases, increasing earnings per share.

Risks

A key risk to Netflix’s investment thesis is a slowdown in consumer spending. As a consumer discretionary service, streaming subscriptions are typically one of the first items customers cut back on during economic distress. The macroeconomic background is unfavorable for the everyday consumer. Persistent inflation, elevated interest rates, and sustained tariff-driven costs all create pressure for many wallets. When the average gallon of gas is $4.08, consumers are more cognizant of their budget and less likely to divert money toward discretionary items such as streaming subscriptions.

In addition, geopolitical tensions from tariff policy directly affect NFLX. A key strategic priority has been tapping international markets for subscriber growth, such as South Korea, Spain, Brazil, and India. Should trade tensions escalate, NFLX could face margin erosion in international markets due to rising production costs, given that approximately 75% of Netflix’s overall content is produced internationally, as per CNBC.

Conclusion

Netflix is approaching 1Q26 earnings from a position of strength. The recent pullback in share price, driven by broader macroeconomic concerns, presents an attractive entry point for long-term investors.

Looking at consensus estimates, we see that NFLX has received 16 downgrades in the past 90 days with only one upgrade. Estimates stand at $0.76 per share, with revenue estimated to be $12.17 billion. There are three key tailwinds going into the print: an increase in subscription prices, accelerating ad revenue supported by a proprietary ad tech stack, and a $2.8 billion WBD breakup fee.

That being said, there is a material risk to NFLX due to consumer wallets feeling pressure and trade tensions potentially causing margin erosion. However, I believe NFLX will beat earnings estimates and raise full-year guidance with its strong tailwinds. As a result, I rate NFLX a Buy.

data.title: Week Ahead: It's Still Mostly About The War
data.description:
The Middle East war remains the dominant fundamental condition. There continues to be some indication that market participants are hopeful of a resolution soon. The May WTI contract settled near $111.55 last week, which appears to build in the risk of near-term escalation, but the June contract settled close to $98. The September contract is below $78. The US S&P 500 rose for the first time in six weeks. Benchmark 10-year yields in the US and Europe eased. Still, the firing of three US Army generals and new attacks on Middle East infrastructure warn that further escalation seems likely before de-escalation.

The week ahead begins slowly with many centers shut for Easter Monday. The highlights include the US and China's CPI and Canada's employment report. The Reserve Bank of New Zealand is the only G10 central bank that meets and is most likely to stand pat. All two dozen economists in Bloomberg's survey expect Poland's central bank to maintain its reference rate at 3.75%. There is also an agreement among economists that the Bank of India will leave its repo rate at 5.25%. India's central bank announced capital controls last week, which limited the size of banks' foreign exchange positions and barred their activity in the non-deliverable forward market to defend the rupee. It may complicate hedging of Indian bonds by international asset managers.

US

Drivers: Optimism that the Middle East war will end shortly saw the dollar's gains pared. The futures market is again pricing in around a 30% chance of a cut this year. At the extreme, on March 26, the market was discounting an almost 58% chance of a hike. Before the war began, the two-year yield settled below 3.40% for the first time in several years. It was around 4.03% a month later. After the March jobs report, it finished last week near 3.82%.

Data: The new week begins with the March ISM services, which are likely to start picking up the disruption caused by the war, with activity slower and prices higher. February durable goods orders and another look at Q4 GDP will be less consequential. February personal income, consumption, and deflators will provide a base for comparison and to see the impact of the war. March CPI at the end of the week will draw attention even though questions have been raised over the increased use of “imputation" by the Bureau of Labor Statistics. The base effect does not augur well. Last March, headline CPI was flat, and the core rose by 0.1%. The FOMC minutes from the meeting earlier this month may offer insight into official thinking two weeks into the war.

Prices: The Dollar Index (DXY) settled last week above 100.00 for the second consecutive week. The high since the war began was recorded last Tuesday near 100.65, its best level since last May. The momentum indicators did not confirm the high, but the risk that the war escalates seems to limit selling interest. A convincing break of 99.00 is needed to boost the odds that a high is in place.

EMU

Drivers: The euro lost nearly four cents amid the position adjustment to war and anticipated economic shock. It reached a low near $1.14 around the ides of March. However, as optimism of a relatively short war took hold, the euro recovered. The US two-year premium over Germany had collapsed to a five-year low near 118 bp (from the year's peak in January of almost 153 bp). The swaps market has about a 50% chance of a hike at the end of this month, down from around 85% chance at its peak.

Data: The eurozone data is unlikely to have much market impact. The final March services and composite PMI may soften further from the preliminary estimates. February retail sales and PPI are unlikely to be important factors for consideration when the ECB meets later this month. Nor will they have much impact on risk appetites, which are being driven by the disruption of the war.

Prices: The euro has spent most of the last several weeks trading between $1.1400 and a little above $1.1600. The momentum indicators bottomed around the middle of March when the euro was approaching the lower end of the range. Still, they are not generating strong signals, and the five- and 20-day moving averages have converged around $1.535-45. If/when the markets see an off-ramp to the war, this broadly sideways price action could mark a base.

PRC

Drivers: Beijing did not take advantage of the US dollar's strength to depreciate the yuan. It has shown restraint and actually set the yuan's fix against the dollar at its highest level in several years last week (CNY6.8880). China is the world's largest importer of oil, and the higher price and disruption in a range of other industries is a challenge for China, but the higher energy prices also will likely boost the demand for EVs and solar panels. Beijing likely gathered a great deal of intelligence on US military operations and tactics, and depth of supply chains.

Data: China may report lending figures in the coming days, but it can be counted on to publish March consumer and producer prices at the end of the week. Even though oil from Iran is still making its way to China, US-Israel war on Iran is likely disruptive. Even before the war began, China was emerging from the consumer deflation. The CPI rose 1.3% year-over-year in February, the most in three years. The deflation in producer prices had lessened in six of the seven months through February and, at -0.9%, was the least since January 2023. The median forecasts in Bloomberg's survey see the PPI emerging from deflation (0.5%) and the CPI little changed at 1.2%.

Prices: While the PBOC has pushed the dollar fix lower, the offshore yuan remains range-bound. The greenback has traded between about CNH6.86 and CNH6.9435 since the Middle East war began. Last week's high, near CNH6.9270, was the highest since March 9. A development worth monitoring is that the onshore yuan has been trading stronger than the offshore yuan, with some notable exceptions, since the war began. It had been trading mostly weaker since late November, which had bolstered our conviction of the underlying trend.

Japan

Drivers: The yen is sensitive to the broad direction of the dollar. The 30-day rolling correlation of changes in the dollar against the yen and the Dollar Index is around 0.72, not far from where it was on the eve of the Middle East war. It bottomed in mid-March below 0.60, and the low for the year was in the third week of January around 0.52. The dollar-yen 30-day correlation with changes in the US 10-year yield is near 0.60, the upper end of where it has been since last October. In January, it had fallen to almost 0.10, the lowest since May 2025. Counterintuitively, over the past 30 sessions, changes in dollar-yen and Japan's two-year yield are positively correlated by the most in two months. This means the rising Japanese short-term yields are associated with a stronger dollar, not yen. Of note, the changes in the dollar-yen and the S&P 500 over the past 30 sessions are the most inverse (~-0.35) since last October. It means that the dollar tends to move higher against the yen when US equities decline (risk-off).

Data: Most of Japan's data is for February, before the US/Israel war on Iran began. These include household spending, labor income, and the current account (which has improved sequentially for the past 20 years in February without fail). March producer prices are due ahead of the weekend. Still, the market is discounting about a 70% chance of a BOJ hike when it meets next on April 28, and about a 60% chance of another one in Q4.

Prices: The dollar is trading choppily but mostly in a JPY158-JPY160 trading range. Both sides of the range have been violated by 0.4-0.5 yen a couple of times since the Ides of March. While Japanese officials have continued to warn about one-way markets and speculation, the conditions have not met their threshold for actual material intervention. The dollar settled lower in three of last week's five sessions. The yen finished stronger last week and has been alternating for the past three weeks between advances and declines. One-month implied volatility eased every day last week and is now (~8.9%) the lowest since the war began.

UK

Drivers: The single most important driver of sterling continues to be the broad direction of the US dollar. The rolling 30-day correlation between changes in sterling and the Dollar Index is inverse, -0.85. It has not been much more extreme this year. Last October, it reached a little beyond -0.90, which was the most since April 2024. Perhaps, it is even simpler. The 30-day correlation of changes in sterling and changes in the euro is about 0.88, the highest since mid-2025.

Data: The final services and composite PMI and the March construction PMI seem of secondary importance, especially for the Bank of England, which meets on April 30. The swaps market is discounting a little more than a 50% chance of a hike, and at least one more before the end of the year (~80% chance of another).

Prices: Sterling looks heavy. At the end of March, it recorded a new low since last November, near $1.3160. It recovered a couple of cents on optimism about the war, but when it faded, sterling was pressed below $1.3200. The momentum indicators are stretched but show no sign that they are poised to turn higher. Perhaps, escalation of the war could see sterling approach the $1.3000-40 technical target. Another weight on sterling is coming from the cross against the euro. In the first two weeks of the war, the euro fell around 2% against sterling, but since the middle of March, the euro has recouped about 1.5% and looks poised to continue to outperform sterling.

Canada

Drivers: The US dollar-Canadian dollar exchange rate is not as sensitive to changes in oil prices as the conventional narrative suggests. Over the past 30 sessions, the correlation has been slightly positive, meaning the Canadian dollar is more likely to weaken in the face of a rise in oil prices than to strengthen. Statistically, it is not significant (less than 0.15), though it is the most since last September, but the sign is notable. The correlation of the greenback against the Canadian dollar and the Dollar Index is a little above 0.60. The peak near 0.85 last month was the highest since May 2024.

Data: The jobs data at the end of the week is more important for investors and policymakers than the survey results (service and composite PMI and IVEY). Canada lost a whopping 108.4k full-time jobs in February, and the unemployment rate rose from 6.5% to 6.7%. Another poor report would likely weigh on the Canadian dollar. The swaps market has less than a 10% chance of a rate hike this month (April 29) and does not have a hike fully discounted until Q4. In recent days the swaps market has pulled back from discounting two or more rate hikes this year, as it had mulled in late March.

Prices: The US dollar reached just beyond CAD1.3965 last week to record a new marginal high since last December. The pullback toward CAD1.3870 may give it a running start to the next technical target in the CAD1.4000-15 area. To be sure, the momentum indicators are stretched after the greenback has run up about 3.25% since the March 9 low. That means that what we expect to be the next leg up will complete the move. We will be especially attentive to reversal price patterns.

Australia

Drivers: The Australian dollar tends to be sensitive to the overall US dollar environment, and over the past 30 sessions, its correlation with changes in the Dollar Index is near -0.75. It has not been much more extreme since Q2 24. There also seems to be a sensitivity to the risk environment. The 30-session correlation with the S&P 500 reached above 0.70 last month, rising from around 0.20 in early February. It is now near 0.62. The correlation with gold peaked around 0.80 last month, the highest since last 2022. It fell to the low for the year near 0.30 in late March and is now around 0.45.

Data: The final March services and composite PMI will be of passing interest. February household spending may attract more attention as the central bank seemed to have responded to the surge in Q4 25 (average of slightly more than 0.6% a month, matching its strongest quarterly performance since Q3 23), with the two rate hikes before the war began. The futures market is pricing in almost an 80% chance of a third consecutive hike.

Prices: The Australian dollar fell to two-month lows last week near $0.6835. The daily momentum indicators are oversold and look poised to turn higher. Yet the price action has not been persuasive that an important low is indeed in place. There may be potential for one more leg down to around $0.6800.

Mexico

Drivers: The peso appears to have two dominant drivers presently. The first is the general direction of the dollar. Changes in the US dollar against the Mexican peso and the Dollar Index are 0.70 correlated over the past 30 sessions, the most since last October. The other driver is the risk appetite. Changes in the US dollar against the Mexican peso and the S&P 500 are inversely correlated (~-0.80), the most since 2020.

Data: The central bank's rate cut in late March and signal that it may cut rates again seem to take some of the thunder away from the March CPI, due Thursday. The headline and core are above the upper end of the 2%-4% target range. Instead, most central bank officials are more concerned about growth. The economy was limping before the war began. At the end of the week, Mexico reports February industrial output. It slumped 1.1% in January, which more than offset the increase in November and December 2025. It was the largest decline in a little more than a year.

Prices: The peso rose by about 1.4% last week, the largest weekly gain since late January. It was the second strongest currency in the region after the Brazilian real (~1.6%). The dollar has been trapped in a narrow range, hugging the 20-day moving average for the past three sessions (~MXN17.8250). The daily momentum indicators are turning lower after the greenback rallied a little more than 5.5% since the war began. It reached almost MXN18.1645 last week, a new high for the year, and stalled in front of the 200-day moving average.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ORR: A Hedge Fund In ETF Clothing, Convincing Buy Opportunity
data.description:
The Militia Long/Short Equity ETF (ORR) is an actively managed, long/short global equity fund that launched on January 14, 2025. It is sub-advised by Militia Investments LLC, with ETF Architect (Empowered Funds) serving as the RIA and PINE Distributors as the distributor. The fund seeks capital appreciation by investing in both long and short positions in equities and ETFs across all market capitalizations, including U.S. and foreign securities. The fund is currently holding 196 positions and charges a management fee of approx. 1.30%. ORR manages approx. $417 million in net assets as of 04/02/2026.

I am initiating ORR as a buy for its exceptional use as a non-correlated sleeve within a diversified portfolio. Since its inception, ORR has returned 47.1% on NAV through February of 2026, which is about 2.5X S&P 500 TR for the same period, all while running structurally lower net market exposure. The fund exploits uncrowded factor anomalies in areas that most U.S. long/short ETFs ignore, carries great fee alignment with the fund manager reinvesting 100% of fee revenue back into shares, and provides low correlation to U.S. equities. There are caveats to this ETF, as this is a levered, single-manager fund with a limited history.

How ORR Works

ORR runs a fundamentally based global stock selection strategy and runs at about 150% long and 100% short. The leverage utilized by ORR gives it characteristics of a hedge fund, but instead of 2/20, it is just a 1.30% management fee. The long section of their book concentrates on developed-market equities outside of the U.S., mainly Japanese small/mid-caps and Mexican infrastructure, while the short side of the book hedges through broad market equity exposure in the form of index ETFs or targets impaired businesses in concentrated positions. The investment strategy that is employed by ORR is well studied, involving low-volatility premium and small-cap value premium. 90% of long exposure also sits outside of the U.S. equity market, which differentiates it from other long/short funds, which largely recycle U.S. mega-cap pair trades.

As far as the holdings of the ETF go, Japan small/mid-cap value (about 40% of long exposure) is the single largest concentration and the clearest expression of the fund's thesis. ORR holds 80+ Japanese equities across industrials, financials, consumer, and technology. Near-zero yen borrowing costs make levered positions attractive when adjusting for funding.

Mexican infrastructure (about 13% of long exposure) includes many airport operators to capture concession economics and embedded pricing power in a relatively inelastic supply/demand market (operators, not leisure travel). U.S. Energy midstream companies comprise about 11% of longs, with built-out asset bases that generate substantial FCF with distribution coverages well above 1.0x. The market tends to undervalue the duration of midstream cash flows due to the stigma and non-cyclicality of the energy sector, which can create a persistent disconnect in fundamental valuations relative to current market prices. Selective U.S. and global longs finish off about 20% of the longs in the fund.

The short book splits into two different parts, with broad index ETF shorts, such as QQQ at -20.2% and IWM at -19.7%. These are mechanical hedging shorts that keep beta at about 0.5, instead of seeking alpha. There is also a short on SDIV at -5.9% that provides a factor hedge against an international value tilt dominating the long side of the portfolio. The shorts that target generating alpha involve targeting BDCs and yield vehicles, which are viewed as chronically over-distributing relative to their sustainable NAV levels. Single-name shorts include names like CVNA at -1% and SMCI at -1%.

The Fee Mechanics

The 14.9% headline ER is misunderstood. 1.30% of this is the management fee, and the remaining 12.89% consists of dividend expenses on shorts, borrowing costs, and margin lending interest accruals. Required accounting entries do not represent economic cost the way a management fee does. When ORR shorts a stock, it must pay that dividend to the lender, but the underlying drops roughly the dividend amount on the ex-date. Borrow costs are also offset by interest on short-sale cash proceeds. The adjusted expense ratio is 1.30%, which is 0.65% per 100% of gross exposure that is managed. Both the PM and the co-founder reinvest all management fee revenue back into ORR shares on margin.

The Performance and Comparison

Through February 28, 2026, ORR generated a YTD +14.4%, 1-year +44.6%, and since inception +47.1% on NAV. ORR does not pay a distribution, and any total return is 100% NAV appreciation. The return drivers involve governance reform and yen carry economics, in addition to Mexican infrastructure traffic growth from nearshoring and US midstream distribution growth with multiple expansions on the long side. Shorts have contributed through BDC NAV erosion and some single-name shorts, though the broad QQQ/IWM NAV has been a decent-sized drag due to strong U.S. equity stretches, but this is by design, as they function as a hedge.

The U.S. long/short equity ETF category is relatively small, containing about 20 funds with a combined AUM of 3.71B and a 1-year average return of approx. 7%.

Metric ORR FTLS BTAL Category Avg
Strategy Global L/S fundamental U.S. L/S quantitative U.S. market neutral anti-beta -
AUM $417M $2.18B $385M $194M
Inception Jan 2025 Sep 2014 Sep 2011 -
Adj. Expense Ratio 1.30% 1.38% 1.65% ~1.5%
1-Year Return +44.6% +8.7% -26.9% +7.0%
Net Long Exposure ~50% ~76% ~0% Varies
Geographic Focus 90%+ non-U.S. U.S. only U.S. only Mostly U.S.
Yield 0.00% 1.20% 2.49% 0.85%
Morningstar Rating - Average (People/Process) - -

FTLS is the largest fund in the category but is a fundamentally different product, being that it is a dampened beta play at 76% net long with U.S.-only quantitative stock selection. It smooths volatility rather than generating diverse alpha. BTAL is the opposite extreme, acting as a pure hedging tool with negative expected returns over time that pays off only during risk-off episodes. ORR occupies a distinct middle ground, being a genuine long/short alpha generation at ~50% net long, but expressed through a global opportunity set, which makes buying ORR so appealing.

In a base case where the S&P 500 returns 8-12% over the next twelve months, ORR's approximate 50% net long and differentiated geographic exposure could reasonably deliver 15-25% total return on NAV, implying a $42-$46 range from the current $36.88. In a U.S. equity drawdown of 15%+, the short book should partially offset long losses, and the fund's 0.5 beta suggests a decline that should be less (about half) than broad market indices.

Downside Risks

Leverage risk is the primary tail concern, as a simultaneous 20% long drawdown and 20% short squeeze produce approximately a 50% loss. Additionally, a BOJ rate normalization is the biggest single threat to a buy thesis. If the BOJ accelerates hikes beyond market expectations, the yen strengthens rapidly and the carry trade unwinds. This was seen in August of 2024 when JPY equities dropped sharply as leveraged carry positions liquidated.

Beyond rates, the governance reform thesis could stall if corporate Japan reverts to entrenchment behavior, cross-shareholding unwinding decelerates, or activist pressure fades. A Japanese recession, which is plausible given demographic headwinds and export sensitivity to China, would hit small AND mid-caps disproportionately hard given their domestic revenue skew.

Liquidity risk is something to consider as well, as many of these names trade thin daily volume, and a forced unwind at scale would face significant market impact. Also, a broader EM or international developed market risk-off event would hit ORR hard given their exposure.

Additionally, it should be noted that Contrarian and alternative investment strategies, such as long/short strategies such as ORR engages in, differ materially from traditional equity investments and risk and are not suitable for all investors, particularly those with short time horizons, low-risk tolerance, or a need for capital preservation. This must be considered when considering ORR or any contrarian strategy as a potential investment. ORR belongs in the non-correlated sleeve alongside managed futures and gold, but never as a core holding of a portfolio.

Final Takeaway

I am rating ORR a buy with a small allocation for diversified portfolios seeking a noncorrelated return sleeve. ORR is the most differentiated product in the long/short ETF category. I see ORR as a genuine alpha-seeking fund, wrapped in an ETF, at a fraction of what it would cost an investor to partake in a hedge fund LP. The risk profile is respectable, but for investors who understand the fund they are investing in, the opportunity to access this kind of strategy at 1.30% is quite uncommon, and this window of opportunity may narrow as their AUM scales.

data.title: Yara International: The Urea Price Shock Everyone Ignores
data.description:
The Strait of Hormuz is often associated with the choked oil flow that has sent oil prices surging. However, what is often ignored is the importance of the region for fertilizer production. In September, I added Yara International (YARIY, YRAIF) to my coverage with a buy rating. Since then, the stock price has climbed 54% against the 67% upside I saw for the stock. So, the upside did not fully materialize, but the new setting regarding the Middle East and urea provides a good moment to assess whether there is additional upside.

Yara Stock Boosted By Urea Shock

The bullish thesis that I initially laid out prior to the war is a simple one. The world population is set to grow in the decades ahead, while arable area will not grow nearly as much and will actually be more or less stable. That drives demand for crop protection, where companies such as FMC (FMC) may play a role. It also drives demand for fertilizers to improve yield. That positions Yara well for the long term even when it is not the biggest producer of fertilizers. Food security is gaining more attention these days, and with food being a basic need, there usually is a strong pass-through mechanism when input costs rise. However, competition from countries with low gas prices, which are a feedstock and energy source for the production of fertilizers, does provide a somewhat challenging backdrop for Yara, which has been experiencing higher gas prices, particularly in Europe.

So, it is not necessarily a no-brainer to invest even if the never ending demand for food is. China is generally seen as the dominant producer of urea, together with India and Russia. We do note that particularly China and India are small exporters of urea, and most of the produced fertilizers are used domestically. So, Yara will not easily sell into those markets, if at all, but in the global fertilizer market, India and China are less meaningful. The top exporters are in the Middle East. Collectively, the countries of Saudi Arabia, Qatar, UAE and Oman provide a significant share of fertilizers for export purposes, as those countries have low natural gas costs, making them extremely competitive, with production exceeding domestic demand.

Rabobank estimates that 25%-30% of the world’s fertilizer for export passes through the Strait of Hormuz. That export flow is now being choked. For Yara, there are two effects. The first one is that due to the higher prices for natural gas, which is the feedstock for ammonia production and turned into urea, the input costs rise along with higher energy costs for production. The higher energy prices may cause some suppliers to exit, along with the supply of fertilizers falling off, creating a situation where supply tightens while demand remains mostly unchanged. So, that provides pricing power. Now, there is a lag effect, as typically contracts do not include pass-through of higher gas costs, but they are passed through on new contracts driven by supply-demand balancing on a new purchase price. Furthermore, we note that higher fertilizer costs may squeeze farmer budgets, which also diminishes some of the pricing power.

So, for Yara, with high gas costs in Europe, the seemingly positive higher global pricing may actually be a negative, as some of its production facilities may have to reduce production due to high input costs. So, I believe that the outlook is actually negative in the short term but positive in the longer term. That also means that while there could be upside in 2026, a resolution of the situation in the Middle East could drive downside from 2026 levels in 2027-2028.

Yara’s Positive Momentum Started Before The War

Share prices of Yara have risen 15% since the start of the war. However, we note that from the start of the year until the start of the war, the stock price had already risen nearly 24%. So, the pre-war surge in the stock price was stronger than the post-war increase in the stock price, and that is likely driven by the fact that Yara was already significantly cutting its costs, driving margins up.

The fourth quarter EBITDA performance shows as much, with EBITDA rising 37% year-on-year and ROIC expanding from 0.5% to 11.4%, driven by better mix, higher pricing, lower energy costs as spot gas prices fell 16% year-on-year prior to the war. Fixed costs were reduced by $31 million, offset by negative currency effects. Gas prices were expected to continue declining in Q1 and Q2. So, that is actually some of the positive momentum that the company took into the first months of 2026. However, the assumption of lower gas prices is no longer valid, and that puts the stock in the situation where the stock price has risen and margins may initially be compressed until existing contracts roll off.

Yara International Stock Still Has Upside

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Yara, the EBITDA performance in 2025 was around 3% better than expected, while 2026 estimates have been lifted by 25.3% and by 10% for 2027 which I believe shows the upward pressure on urea prices driven by the war. For free cash flow, we note that free cash flow in 2025 came in around 15% below expectations, but expectations for 2026 improved by 34% and by 27% for 2027. However, we note that this does not imply that the expectation is continued growth. The increase in the estimates indicates that EBITDA and free cash flow are going to be lower year-on-year in 2027 but at a higher level than previously expected. I believe that the stock price should be around $74.49 indicating 29% upside, and then we are ignoring the upside that 2026 earnings seem to suggest for the simple reason that these levels do not seem to be sustainable. Of course, the picture will change if the war in the Middle East remains a prolonged one with fertilizer exports from the Middle East remaining low.

The key metrics also show that revenues are expected to rise just like EBITDA, but the current expectation is that the 2026 levels will not be sustained in 2027 and 2028. We also see that back in the free cash flow performance, which is expected to be lower in 2027 compared to 2026 and remain steady in 2028. Capital intensity is set to increase when measured against revenues. What I do like about the company is that it is in the position to substantially reduce debt while maintaining a strong cash position, and the net debt leverage is already a strong one.

Conclusion: Yara International Has Upside On Middle East Disruption

Yara International has upside driven by the export reduction in the Middle East reducing supply on the global markets. That provides the company with some pricing power but with a lag effect and the complication that farmer budgets could ultimately squeeze budgets and provide a cap on how much premium can be harvested due to the supply disruption. The fertilizer market is largely self-balancing, and with higher input and energy costs, Yara has upside, but it is not the company with the lowest-cost access to gas. So, there are many factors to keep in mind, but I remain bullish on the stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Occidental Petroleum: Why This Warren Buffett Stock Has A Lot More Upside
data.description:
Occidental Petroleum (OXY) is a major oil and gas producer and one of Warren Buffett’s biggest investments in the past decade. OXY's stock performance has largely been flat in the past few years, which makes it intriguing. Is Buffett seeing something that the market is missing again?

I did not comment on OXY when the latest Middle East conflict started on February 28, 2026; like many others, I was unsure of what to make of it. Without getting too much into the politics, by now it seems more likely that this conflict will fester for a long time and will not end anytime soon. So I’m revisiting OXY as a potential investment—if oil & energy are good long-term investments, then why not start with the stock Buffett bought?

1. When All Eggs in One Basket is Actually Better

During the past twenty or so years when globalization was going well, being globalized had diversification and growth advantages. Starbucks (SBUX) or Apple (AAPL) could reach billions of new customers around the world, say in Russia, China, or the Middle East, generating massive growth and also diversifying across regions.

But ever since COVID, diversification across regions has become more and more of a negative. Supply chain “disruptions” are becoming more and more frequent, and the current Middle East conflict could create even more disruptions if it lasts several months (or even years), ranging from the obvious (oil) to the less obvious (helium, semiconductors, fertilizer, food, aluminum, data centers, just to give a few examples), etc.

If the crisis lasts long enough, it may irreparably shift supply chains for decades to come. Sure, it may be more cost-efficient to source low-cost natural gas and helium in the Middle East, but when semiconductor factories are idle because of a lack of helium and hundreds of billions of orders are unfulfilled or cancelled and even market share is lost to those with natural resources, then sourcing your supplies close to home starts making a lot of sense.

OXY has the overwhelming majority of its revenues generated in the US (85% of its oil revenues). Its international operations are mainly located in Oman (which conveniently does not need to transit the Strait of Hormuz). While time will tell if Oman is eventually impacted by the conflict, in the short run, the action seems to be concentrated in the countries near the Strait. This means that almost all of OXY’s revenues are “conflict-proof.” Even in the extreme case where shipping lanes across the world have issues, OXY will still be able to fall back on the 70% of its revenues being generated by production and sales in the US. This enables OXY to have far higher certainty in its revenues and cash flows, especially in an increasingly unpredictable world where events that a decade ago would be “black swans” are occurring with more regularity.

If we look at OXY through this lens, it’s in a much more favorable position compared to other major oil stocks. For example, Exxon Mobil (XOM) has only 40% of its sales from the US (source: XOM 10-K, US sales as % of total). In the next section we’ll look at whether OXY’s valuation makes sense.

2. Valuations Are Moderate Even if Oil Prices Stay Just Moderately High

In the previous section we discussed the macro case behind investing in OXY. But even with a plausible macro setting, the numbers still need to make sense at the micro level.

If we look at OXY’s financials from 2017, there are a few observations that pop out immediately:

Source: Revenue and net income from Seeking Alpha, average realized oil price from 10-K

Firstly, we can see the earnings are quite cyclical; in some years, net income is several billion dollars or less, and there have been some big losses in 2020 and some big profits in 2022. 2024 and 2025 have been decidedly less impressive, with only $2-3 billion a year in net income.

Secondly, we can see the earnings are quite highly correlated with oil prices. I’m only showing the average realized oil price in the US because that’s where most of the revenues are. Even though OXY also produces oil internationally, I’m assuming the fluctuations are largely in line with US oil prices. I’m not showing natural gas separately because the natural gas price in the US is relatively flat across the decade and is not a major driver of OXY’s performance. At $60/bbl and $64/bbl, OXY earned $2.3 billion in 2021 and 2025, respectively. At $74-76/bbl, OXY’s net profit was $4.7 billion and $3 billion in 2023 and 2024, respectively, whereas it took only $90/bbl oil to achieve a $13 billion net profit in 2022.

To look at it another way, revenue increases pretty much pass through to the bottom line. For example, revenue increased by $10.6bn in 2022, and net profit increased by $10.7bn, while revenues declined by, say, $1.2bn in 2024 and net profit declined by $1.7 billion.

Now if we assume that the net profit increases linearly with oil prices:

Note on formula: Incremental net profit = oil price / $64 (the 2025 average) * $21.6bn (2025 revenues) * 80% (roughly 20% of revenues are non-oil (e.g., chemicals, natural gas, etc., including the Oxy chemical operations recently sold to Berkshire Hathaway)) * (1-30%) (assuming an effective tax rate of about 30%). Total net profit = incremental net profit + 2025 net profit.

At $100 oil, OXY could earn around $10 bn a year (compared to a market cap of $62 bn); it’s roughly a P/E of 6, while at $200 oil, OXY could earn its money back in 2 years. Even at a more moderate $80/oil, OXY could earn $5.7bn/yr, which is not cheap but also not very expensive either at a P/E of 11.

Though OXY has only 10 years of proven oil reserves at current production, it has been able to discover new oil and improve recovery so that reserves are not declining as much. In fact, net declines in 2024 and 2025 have been merely several dozen million barrels, compared with nearly 1.8 billion barrels of proven oil reserves in the US. Assuming this rate of discovery and improved recovery continues, it’s plausible that OXY can keep its oil reserves going for two decades (probably more).

Movement in OXY US proven oil reserves

3. Price is Not Far From Where Warren Buffett Bought

Warren Buffett’s appreciation for OXY has a lot of media coverage. Below we see Berkshire Hathaway buying and nibbling purchases from 2022 up to now, mainly around $50/share, which is not that far from where we are today ($62/share). So though OXY has seen a strong rally in recent weeks, an investor buying now would be buying relatively near Warren Buffett levels.

In fact, OXY is one of Buffett’s biggest investments in the past decades (in terms of cash deployed rather than current fair market value, which would make AAPL seem a lot larger than the actual initial investment deployed in AAPL), and Berkshire currently owns about 32% of OXY’s outstanding shares.

Risks to Bullish Thesis

Unfortunately, how this investment plays out will largely be impacted by oil prices in the next few years. If the current crisis is peacefully resolved in the near future and everything goes back to the way it was and oil goes back to $60/barrel and OXY reverts to $3bn/year earnings, then there might not be much of an investment story.

Another risk is that government policies could reduce how much OXY could benefit from higher oil prices, e.g., via banning energy exports to keep domestic energy prices low or taxing so-called “windfall” profits or any other of the myriad methods that governments use to transfer profits from corporations to state coffers.

Conclusion

I believe OXY is a good buy at these prices, as OXY’s valuations are moderate even if oil prices stay moderately high (e.g., $80/barrel) and could go up a lot more if the current conflict persists. In this era of turbulence, I think there will eventually be a premium on production and sales happening in one country rather than production, sales, and supply chains strewn around the globe and getting impacted by one black swan after another. Plus, the current stock price at $62/share is not that far from Warren Buffett’s entry price in the past three years. Eventually the markets may prove Warren Buffett prescient again.

data.title: Cracker Barrel: Sharp Traffic Declines And Severe Margin Erosion
data.description:
2026 has been an especially volatile year in the stock markets, but at the same time, for consumer-facing businesses, much of the retrenchment in share prices is very much driven by fundamentals. Restaurants, in particular, are having trouble convincing diners to eat out amid sharp menu price inflation, and most restaurant chains are reporting negative comp sales.

Cracker Barrel (CBRL), the diner-style chain that sparked furor last year over a scrapped logo change, is no exception. Over the past year, shares of Cracker Barrel have lost ~20% of their value as sales have contracted. That said, the stock is up modestly since January, an outlier among restaurant chains, as the company's recent earnings print included a modest boost to guidance. The question for investors now is, has Cracker Barrel bottomed, or is this just a dead-cat bounce?

I last wrote a sell article on Cracker Barrel in December, when the stock was trading closer to $27 per share. Since then, Cracker Barrel has actually outperformed the crash in the rest of the stock market, though to be fair, the stock had also been deeply pummeled in 2025 (losing two-thirds of its value after the logo change scandal). With no evidence that fundamentals have any chance of a turnaround, I'm reiterating my sell rating on the stock - in my view, Cracker Barrel's brief outperformance in 2026 isn't likely to last long.

To me, these are the core red flags that investors should be wary about in this stock:

  • Persistently weak traffic trends. Cracker Barrel is suffering ~10% declines in traffic. Right now, its comp sales trends are being held up by higher menu pricing (which is a ~4 point bump to comp sales). As the company laps these menu price increases in calendar 2026, we expect comp sales to deteriorate further.
  • Tarnished brand image. Cracker Barrel suffered a massive blowback in public opinion when the company attempted and then reversed its decision to change its logo in 2025 (even drawing attention from President Trump). Since then, consistently worsening sales trends suggest that the company has alienated a large chunk of its customer base.
  • Entering into a highly promotional restaurant environment. Competitors, especially in the fast-food arena, are leaning more into value meals in order to encourage dining out at a time when consumers feel pinched. Cracker Barrel has also responded by releasing its own value meals and stepping up its promotions. In the long haul, this will have the impact of offsetting the company's price increases and will weaken its margins.
  • Commodity and labor inflation are eating into adjusted EBITDA. It's not just the need to offer deeper promotions that are a major risk to margins. The company is also expecting low-/mid-single-digit inflation in raw commodities and hourly wage labor, which is positioning the stock to decline to a low-single-digit adjusted EBITDA margin - a low even among restaurant peers.
  • Strained balance sheet. The company has very little cash remaining and over $0.5 billion in debt, which sits at a >5x leverage ratio against the company's expected adjusted EBITDA.

In my view, this is a company with a very limited path back into the market's good graces. Its only potential upside is a relatively cheap valuation. At current share prices near $29, Cracker Barrel trades at a market cap of $645.7 million. Netting off the $8.6 million of cash and $531.4 million of debt on the company's latest balance sheet yields an enterprise value of $1.17 billion.

For FY26 (the year for Cracker Barrel ending in July 2026), the company has slightly upped its guidance to $3.24-$3.27 billion in sales, which is a tighter range but with a slightly higher $3.255 billion midpoint that implies a -6.5% y/y sales decline.

The bigger updates to the company's outlook are slightly lower than expected inflation, with commodity inflation now expected at 2.0-2.5% (75bps lower at the midpoint) and wage inflation now expected at 2.5-3.0% (also 75bps lower at the midpoint). This has resulted in a boost in the company's adjusted EBITDA range of $85-$100 million, or a 2.8% margin at the midpoint and slightly higher than the midpoint of the previously wider $70-$110 million range.

This positions Cracker Barrel's valuation at 12.6x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA. This is by no means expensive, but when we consider that other struggling restaurant chains like Wendy's (WEN) and Restaurant Brands International (QSR), the parent of Burger King, are trading at in-line or lower multiples, we find Cracker Barrel entirely not compelling.

Continue to steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

Q2 Recap

Let's now go through Cracker Barrel's fiscal Q2 (January quarter) results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Cracker Barrel's revenue declined -8% y/y to $874.8 million, nevertheless slightly outperforming Wall Street's expectations of $864.3 million (-9% y/y) by a one-point margin.

The big issue here remains the company's comp sales trends, which, as a reminder, measure the revenue growth from continuously open stores and exclude the contribution of newly opened or recently closed locations. Comp restaurant sales in Q2 declined -7.1%, which worsened relative to a -4.7% decline in Q1 (and was substantially worse relative to 5.4% growth in Q4).

Again, as previously mentioned, higher menu pricing helped to blunt the impact of a ~10% decline in traffic. Management optimistically noted that traffic trends improved throughout the quarter and declined "only" -9% in January; still, we have little confidence amid a shaky macro that Cracker Barrel can truly recover to positive traffic.

Speaking to detailed traffic trends on the Q2 earnings call, CFO Craig Pommells noted as follows:

Comparable store restaurant sales decreased by 7.1%, which included a traffic decline of 10.1%. From a monthly perspective, November and December traffic both declined between 10% and 11%. We were encouraged by the improvement in January, which declined 9%, including an approximately 50 basis point net year-over-year unfavorable impact from weather.

Restaurant average check increased 3.4% and included pricing of 4.2%. Menu mix was negative, driven primarily by higher discounts. Off-premise sales were 23.6% of restaurant sales, which increased modestly over prior year. Total retail revenue decreased 9.3% to $180.5 million, and comparable store retail sales decreased by 9.2%."

Core to the company's strategy to pull customers back into Cracker Barrel stores is a heightened focus on price. In the fall, the company launched a 2-for-1 meal deal priced at $19.99, available on weekdays and offering two main courses alongside sides or desserts. The company is also trying to drive upsell via low-priced add-ons, including extra sides and $5 for a soup and salad add-on.

Still, we note that the cost burden of promotions, on top of diseconomies of scale from a shrinking comp sales base, eroded the company's margins. Adjusted EBITDA in Q2 clocked in at $38.2 million, down -49% y/y.

Adjusted EBITDA margins of 4.4% also eroded -350bps y/y versus 7.9% margins in the year-ago Q2. For a company shouldering a debt burden at a 5.7x leverage ratio ($522.8 million in debt net of cash, divided by $92.5 million in midpoint expected EBITDA this year), the sharp deterioration in margins bears additional scrutiny.

Key Takeaways

With decelerating comp sales, sharply declining traffic, eroding margins, and a huge debt load, there's little to like about Cracker Barrel. Yes, it's true that the company slightly lifted its (already downcast) outlook for FY26, but I fail to see any appeal in this stock at ~13x adjusted EBITDA when the company's EBITDA profile is in a sharp y/y decline. Avoid this stock and invest elsewhere.

data.title: XPEL: Why This Stock Could Deliver Strong Upside
data.description:
Investment Thesis

At first glance, XPEL Inc. (XPEL) looks expensive and trades at a premium compared to peers. But in my view, that premium is justified as the fundamentals of the underlying business continue to improve. Based on my analysis, the revenue has the potential to grow, driven by the acquisition of China assets, EBITDA margin recovery, strong FCF generation, a unique business model with the DAP platform and a company-owned installation center, and a growing addressable market. Furthermore, it is trading at almost half its own 5-year historical EV/EBITDA average. As margins recover to historical levels and increase in revenue from China operations, there is a reasonable case for the valuation multiple to move higher from current levels.

The company is a global provider of protective films and coatings for automotive protection. Its products include paint protection films, window film, ceramic coating, and windshield protection film. In terms of channel strategy, it sells products through aftermarket, dealership, OEM, and e-commerce.

China Distribution Acquisition: Has Potential to Accelerate Revenue Growth

Before the distribution acquisition in China, the operations were managed through a 3rd-party distribution channel partnership. From a business perspective, this was not adding full value to the operations, as XPEL had limited control over pricing decisions, inventory management, or channel development. Through this acquisition, the company can run operations independently now. This is a big stock catalyst, as XPEL now has access to the large automotive market. According to the CAAM analysis of 2025, China maintained the top global position in automobile production, and sales reached 34.5 million and 34.4 million units, respectively.

To understand the significance of this deal, we have to evaluate the latest quarterly results, which are the first full quarter post-acquisition of China distribution assets. The contribution from China significantly increased by 65.3% to $39.9 million, and its share of total revenue increased from 5.7% in FY 2024 to 8.4% in FY 2025. The change in contribution to total revenue is significant given its revenue from just a single quarter, and on a full-year basis, this will be higher in the future. With direct operational control, the growth opportunity from China is significantly larger than what the investors expected from this deal. Although the stock is trading at a premium to peers, given this structural change in channel strategy, I believe the stock can further outperform and deserves a higher valuation.

In a nutshell, the investor should track this revenue growth, and, given the size of the China market, the stock has a potential for further substantial upside.

Paint Protection Film Market: Competitive Strength Within a Large Addressable Market

XPEL competes in the paint protection film market against 3M (MMM), Eastman Chemical Company (EMN), Avery Dennison (AVY), and Saint-Gobain (CODYY). All these are diversified companies with much broader product offerings compared to XPEL. However, XPEL is a purely focused player with leaner operations to design products as per the requirements of the industry.

The DAP (Design Access Platform) software platform from XPEL is the key differentiating unique factor that sets it apart from all competitors. This platform is used by 90,000 certified installers who pay monthly access fees to design and cut paint protection film to perfectly fit as per the specific vehicle models. Once installers are trained on the DAP platform and include it in their operations, it's not easy to switch to a competitor. It's the same if we are using Microsoft Suite for workflow; then moving to another platform is not easy and involves switching costs too. Further, the company also operates company-owned installation centers and has a dedicated dealership service, which is different compared to other players. In FY 2025, service revenue consists of 24% of total revenue, which includes DAP platform fees, installation labor from company-owned installation centers, and dealership services. In a nutshell, this recurring revenue base is a unique advantage over competitors who sell only products. For investors, it's important to understand this unique proposition by having a business model consisting of product and service offerings. Such business models should deserve a premium valuation too.

According to Grand View Research, the global paint protection film market was estimated at $502.55 million in 2024 and is estimated to reach $726.63 million by 2030, growing at a CAGR of 6.6%. Within Asia-Pacific, China will be the largest market where XPEL has direct operations now. Other predictions from Mordor Intelligence and Research and Markets suggest growth of 5.41% and 7.16%, respectively. Based on all three estimates, we can conclude that XPEL is operating in the industry with positive growth, as the market is growing by 5–7% annually over the next 5 years. The revenue growth of XPEL is 13.3% in FY 2025, which indicates strong performance by generating double the industry average growth rate. I believe with the inclusion of China, the revenue growth will further accelerate in the next few years.

In a nutshell, XPEL has competitive strength through its DAP platform and company-owned installation centers and operates within a large addressable market expected to grow by 5–7% annually over the next 5 years.

EBITDA Margin on Path of Recovery

The EBITDA margin analysis is important for understanding the margin recovery, as annual and quarterly margins signal different trends. By analyzing the quarterly data, we understand the recovery in the underlying business with improvement in margin.

Data in $ million FY 2023 FY 2024 FY 2025 Q4 2024 Q4 2025
Revenue 396.3 420.4 472.2 107.5 122.3
Growth 6.1% 12.3% 13.8%
Gross Margin 42.2% 42.2% 40.6% 41.9%
EBITDA 76.9 69.5 77.4 14.3 19.6
Margin 19.4% 16.5% 16.4% 13.3% 16.0%

Data calculated by the author from FY 2025 10-K and Q4 2025 Earnings Presentation

In FY 2025, the gross margin was at 42.2%, which is similar to FY 2024. However, the EBITDA margin decreased slightly from 16.5% to 16.3% due to operating expenses increasing by 17.1% in FY 2025. The higher expenses were driven primarily by expenses associated with the China acquisition. In other words, the margin compression was the result of one-off expenses due to M&A and not a deterioration. On a quarterly basis, the situation is more encouraging for the investors. In Q4 2025, the EBITDA margin was 16.0%, compared to 13.3% in Q4 2024, up by 37.6% year-over-year. What's the reason for this sudden improvement? The drag in 2024 was due to inventory issues with the OEM, an escalation in operating expenses to support international expansion, and dealership. By Q4 2025, all three factors had been improved, resulting in margin recovery. For investors, it's important to understand that the company is operationally strong and withstands the industry cycle. To substantiate this, management has guided that gross margins are expected to improve in Q2 2026.

For comparison, the sector operates at median EBITDA margins of 11.01%. Whereas, XPEL already has margins above sector level and has a clear path to recovery toward a 5-year average margin of the 17 to 18% range. This is an important indicator for investors because the cost structure was increased by a temporary factor, not a structural issue with the business.

Strong Cash Generation Confirms Earnings Quality

Data in $ million FY 2023 FY 2024 FY 2025
Cash From Operations 37.4 47.8 66.9
Growth 27.8% 40.0%
Capex 6.3 6.7 4.0
Free Cash Flow (CFO-Capex) 31.1 41.1 62.9
FCF Growth 32.2% 53.0%
EBITDA 76.9 69.5 77.4
Cash From Operations as % of EBITDA 48.6% 68.8% 86.4%

Data calculated by the author from FY 2025 10-K and Q4 2025 Earnings Presentation

Before we move to the latest financials, let me share an important historical trend. The company generated the highest cash flow in FY 2025. Further, in the last 10 years, all years had positive cash flows. For a company that is operating in a cyclical automotive industry, this is exceptional performance and resilience of the business model.

In FY 2025, the cash from operations reached $66.9 million, an increase of 40% from $47.8 million in FY 2024. This represents a very high conversion ratio of 86%. For comparison, the conversion ratio was 69% in FY 2024 and 49% in FY 2023. Why is this conversion ratio important for investors to track? The continuous improvement in cash conversion is significant because it shows profits translating into actual cash due to business efficiency.

Further, FCF increased by 53% to $62.9 million in FY 2025. The balance sheet is clean with negligible debt and gives management significant decision-making options for business operations. In this case, with $62.9 million in FCF and no meaningful debt, the company has the flexibility to invest in channel strategy, pursue inorganic growth, and return capital to shareholders. According to management, further strategic investments in manufacturing and the supply chain are planned for 2026. Based on this, we can conclude that the balance sheet provides strong support for both investment and shareholder returns as revenue accelerates. For investors, this is a unique proposition to pick a stock that has strong fundamentals and is almost debt-free.

Valuation

Peers EV/EBITDA (Forward)
XPEL, Inc. (XPEL) 13.31x
Dorman Products, Inc. (DORM) 9.06x
PHINIA Inc. (PHIN) 6.34x
BorgWarner Inc. (BWA) 6.02x
Genuine Parts Company (GPC) 9.85x
Peers Average 8.92x
Sector Median 9.44x
XPEL 5-Year Average 24.44x

Sourced from Seeking Alpha

XPEL is currently trading at a 49% premium to the peer average of 8.92x based on the forward EV/EBITDA multiple. Although this looks expensive at first glance, a detailed fundamental analysis explains a different and much more positive investment thesis. The company is growing at double-digit revenue growth with a software-based unique model, a direct distribution channel strategy, and significant international expansion. With such a situation, I believe stocks trading at a premium are justified.

For investors, the more interesting fact is the stock is currently trading at almost half its historical average EV/EBITDA multiple. As margins recover to historical levels and increase in revenue from China operations, there is a reasonable case for the valuation multiple to move higher from current levels.

However, investors have to closely watch the recovery in margin, integration of China operations, and automobile demand. As a delay in margin recovery or integration issue in China could be a negative catalyst for the stock.

Final Thoughts

XPEL is a strong company growing revenue at double the industry growth rate over the next 5 years, recovering margin with movement to higher historical margin, generating strong free cash flow, and crucially integrating the China story. I suggest waiting for Q1 2026 results to confirm the margin recovery direction and more data on China operations before initiating a position. For existing investors, this is a long-term investment thesis and reasonable to stick with investment with the caveat of broader automobile sector demand.

data.title: AngioDynamics Growing, But With More Noise Than The Street Likes
data.description:
AngioDynamics (ANGO) has been looking more and more like a “two steps forward, one and a half steps back” company. Management has inarguably made progress in building the company’s growth-oriented Medical Tech business and boosted the company’s overall growth profile, but quarter-to-quarter performance has remained erratic and unpredictable, and progress in margin expansion has likewise been unsteady.

AngioDynamics shares are down about 20% since my last update, with two sizable selloffs after the last two quarterly earnings reports tied to growth, margins/reinvestment, and perhaps the pending retirement of the CEO.

At this point I do think AngioDynamics shares are likely undervalued, but this is not a stock for investors who get anxious about short-term market reactions. The Med Tech business does appear to be on solid footing, with sizable addressable markets ahead, but there’s still a lot of work to do before these shares will reliably sustain a better multiple.

Another Quarter of “Mixed” Trends

AngioDynamics’ third quarter offered food for both the bulls and bears, with the latter ultimately winning out on weaker guidance for fourth quarter EBITDA.

Revenue rose 9%, beating by about 2%, with the Med Tech business growing 19% (following 13% growth in FQ2, 26% growth in FQ1, and 22% growth in FQ4’25) and the Medical Devices business growing about 1%.

AlphaVac continues to perform well off of a small base, logging 47% growth on increased usage in pulmonary embolisms. AngioVac saw 5% growth but a small sequential decline. Auryon rose 18%, which is strong against an underlying market growing in the low double-digits, but I’d frankly been hoping that this business would be consistently growing at 20%+ by now. NanoKnife revenue rose 21%, with probe revenue up 20% (and about 4% sequentially), as usage grows in the wake of FDA approval for prostate cancer and a new CPT code that simplifies reimbursement.

Gross margin took a meaningful step back this quarter, with overall gross margin declining 110bp yoy and 350bp qoq largely due to $1.3M in new tariff costs. Med Tech gross margin was stable yoy at 62.5% but down 280bp qoq, while Med Device margin was 44.2% vs. 47.4% a year ago and 49.1% in the prior quarter.

Adjusted EBITDA improved 38% year over year, though the margin remains around 2% (well below most “quality growth” med-techs).

Weak Guidance for Q4, but Not Necessarily for Bad Reasons

All told, revenue performance was pretty good. I’m encouraged by the strong uptake in AlphaVac, though slightly disappointed in Auryon (which has more to do with my expectations/hopes than true underperformance). NanoKnife is a tougher call. It seems churlish to be disappointed with 20%+ growth, but I would like to see more momentum in probes.

On the flip side, there’s work to do on margins. The tariff situation is an “is what it is” situation, particularly as a company of AngioDynamics’ size doesn’t have a lot of mitigation options in the short term. Likewise, there’s just not enough scale in the business yet to fully offset needed reinvestment in SG&A and R&D, though I think more could likely be done to improve (or sell) the remaining Medical Device businesses.

Management’s guidance for the fourth quarter didn’t go over well with the market. While revenue guidance was raised again (and is about 2% higher than the initial guidance for FY’26), and gross margins were guided stable, EBITDA guidance implies a pretty weak fourth quarter.

To that end, management did confirm increased spending, largely on the clinical side. Likewise, management indicated that they’d be using cash to build inventory ahead of some pre-announced business interruptions at the company’s sterilization service provider.

Wall Street is a “what have you done for me lately?” sort of place, but I don’t see this guidance as particularly egregious. Management has the upcoming APEX-RETURN and PAVE studies to fund, both of which could expand the use (and revenue) of AlphaVac and AngioVac, respectively, and there is an ongoing study for Auryon in below-the-knee peripheral atherectomy (AMBITION BTK). I regard these as “takes money to make money” propositions, and arguably the Street should have been expecting an uptick in R&D spending.

As for the inventory build, what is the alternative? It’s impractical to switch sterilization providers on short notice, and it makes sense to me to build inventory ahead of a known disruption as a means of avoiding running out of stock and alienating customers (and/or forcing them to switch to competing products).

The Outlook

My expectations were lower than the Street’s, so not much of note happens with my model at this point. My revenue expectations are a bit higher now, but I’m still looking for around 6% annualized revenue growth over the next five years and long-term growth of over 5%.

Frankly, as the Med Tech business gains scale, I won’t be surprised to see AngioDynamics exit more of its legacy businesses. Between minimal revenue growth and weaker gross margins, there aren’t a lot of arguments for keeping them once the Med Tech business has scaled up.

As far as margins go, this remains a work in progress, particularly given the likelihood (in my view, at least) that management will seek to launch additional studies to expand the labels and marketing appeal of its core growth businesses (AlphaVac, Auryon, and NanoKnife). I think a mid-to-high single-digit EBITDA margin is still possible in FY’27, and I do think a double-digit margin within three years is likewise possible.

Valuation remains challenging, due in large part to the fact that the company isn’t growing fast enough for the Street to ignore the margin situation. With that, the Medical Device business effectively drags down the Med Tech business; I can argue for a $16-$17/share fair value based on a 2x multiple that blends a roughly 4x multiple for the Med Tech business with a sub-1x multiple for Medical Devices.

An upcoming CEO transition is also relevant to a discussion of AngioDynamics’ outlook. CEO Jim Clemmer, who has served for around 10 years now, has announced his intention to retire in FY’27, and the company is now searching for a replacement.

AngioDynamics shares are down about 20% over Clemmer’s tenure, but I would argue the business is on better footing now with the sale of multiple legacy businesses (where the company didn’t have bright growth/margin prospects) and with the repositioning around AngioVac/AlphaVac, Auryon, and NanoKnife. In any case, I’m curious to see who the board selects for the role, and I would expect that those growth businesses will remain the core of the business going forward.

The Bottom Line

My biggest issue with AngioDynamics is honestly the fact that there are a lot of investment options in the med-tech space, and many of them offer less messy, or at least less volatile, growth and value stories. That said, I do think that AngioDynamics is on the right track, and I do think the shares don’t fully reflect the potential of the growth business. For patient investors, I still see a worthwhile case for owning this stock, but that word “patient” is a key consideration here.

data.title: Oracle: OCI And Multicloud Execution Shift The Narrative To Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Since my last coverage, which I rated as Sell, Oracle Corp. (ORCL) stock is down about 24% until 2 April 2026, as investors continue to have concerns about the company’s heavy asset-intensive business model regarding the Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) business line and its high debt leverage.

ORCL remains with negative FCF, and its backlog depends on OpenAI; however, Q3’26 results showed that the company, on the one hand, reduced OpenAI’s dependency and, on the other hand, proved that the OCI business accelerates the overall growth trajectory, as well as being a key component for cross-selling opportunities leading to customer stickiness.

OCI Accelerates Oracle’s Growth Story via Revenue Acceleration but Also Through Business Restructuring

Oracle’s total revenue rose by 22% YoY to $17.2B in Q3’26, with the main driver being the Cloud segment, which grew by 44%. Specifically, Cloud growth came mainly from its AI infrastructure business, which grew by 84%, while applications grew a solid 13%.

As you can see, ORCL’s total revenue growth rate is strongly correlated with the Cloud segment, which shows a strong growth rate acceleration over the last two years due to high AI demand and, in my opinion, will continue to record higher rates in the following quarters, given the company’s guidance of 46%-50% in Q4’26.

The OCI story does not stop here. According to the CRN report, the OCI segment will have a 75% CAGR, broken down as follows:

  • Revenue of $34 in FY27
  • Revenue of $77B in FY28
  • Revenue of $129B in FY29
  • Revenue of $166B in FY30

Investors remain concerned due to Cloud growth coming with lower profitability, driven by the need for high capital investments mainly for GPUs, CPUs, and memory. Specifically, cloud operating profit of $8.7B in Q3’26 reflects an operating margin of 58%, which is slightly lower than the operating margin of 63% in Q3’25.

In contrast, Hardware and Service segments reported operating margins of 68% and 28% in Q3’26, respectively, compared to 64% and 20% in the previous year. Given that Cloud is the company’s main segment, overall segment-based operating margin reached 56% in Q3’26, down from 59% in Q3’25, but still at high levels.

The company’s overall operating margin increased to 32% in the last quarter, from almost 31% in the previous year, mainly due to cost efficiency improvements and reductions in R&D, administrative, and restructuring expenses.

That said, the company on March 31, 2026, announced layoffs of about 20,000-30,000 employees, which could lead to $8-$10B in free cash flow. I suggest investors should not be concerned about this because Oracle, like many other tech companies, replaced personnel in specific departments with AI tools.

Teams that were affected most are Revenue and Health Sciences (RHS) and SaaS and Virtual Operations Services (SVOS), with at least 30% job cuts per team. RHS is a team that develops software for the health industry, while the SVOS team manages SaaS solutions, such as ERP or HCM. In simple terms, Oracle fired developers and will use AI to produce code -a recently common cost-reduction strategy among many top-tier tech companies.

One more positive sign from the last quarter is the company’s backlog. Specifically, Remaining Performance Obligations (RPO) ended at $553B, up 325% YoY, related to large-scale AI contracts where the company does not expect to raise any additional funds to support these contracts. The key point, in my view, is that ORCL seems to gradually reduce its high concentration on OpenAI with new large companies.

Oracle Shifted Toward a Capital-Heavy IaaS Business Model, but Multicloud (Database) Solutions Could Reach Clients Outside Its Ecosystem

The company shifted its business model from a SaaS asset‑light business model towards a capital‑heavy IaaS one. This shift is reflected in high debt and CapEx, with a direct impact on FCF. Oracle continues to report negative FCF, given its high CapEx of $48B in Q3’26, and the company raised its expectation for the full year from $35B to $50B. I see that ORCL’s high revenue growth is leveraged with high debt -about $162B – due to massive investments, as I mentioned in my previous article.

But in the last quarter, the company stated something that changed my view:

Multicloud Database revenue grew 531% year-over-year. AI infrastructure revenue grew 243% year-over-year. Both also have demand that exceeds supply and a clear execution plan from Oracle that will rapidly turn that demand into profitable recurring revenue. We created our Multicloud partnerships with first Microsoft, then Google and finally, Amazon to bring the best database platform to all clouds… We have global region coverage in all of our partner clouds. We now have 33 regions that live with Microsoft and 14 live with Google. We delivered significant growth with AWS beginning Q3 with 2 AWS regions live, exiting Q3 with 8 AWS regions live, and we will exit Q4 with 22 AWS regions live… as I mentioned earlier, about our Multicloud Database business, that's a much higher margin business, more in the 60% to 80% range.

Let me first explain what Multicloud is based on Oracle's definition:

Multicloud is the coordinated use of cloud services from more than one cloud service provider. By using multiple providers, companies can avoid being tied down to one vendor and maintain their flexibility.

The company also highlights the benefits of using multicloud as follows:

Also, customers can utilize the best product from one cloud service provider and pair it with the best product from another cloud service provider, getting the best‑fit solution.

The key point here is that ORCL offers native cross-cloud solutions. Whereas Microsoft (MSFT) and Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL) offer multicloud solutions by containerized workload mobility, ORCL co-allocates its own hardware -such as CPUs, GPUs, and storage servers - within other vendors' data centers.
